message:
Add sizing benchmark for data volume study (wa-4vx.3.11)

Benchmark measures insert throughput, DB growth, and query latency
at various scales. Results show RETENTION ONLY is sufficient for v0.1/v0.2:
- Insert: ~7K segments/sec (7x above budget)
- 30-day retention keeps DB under 10GB for typical use

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.14","title":"E2E script: wezterm user-var → wezterm.lua forwards → `wa event` → watcher receives signal","description":"# Task: E2E script — user-var forwarding lane

## Goal
Prove the user-var lane works end-to-end in a real WezTerm instance.

## Scenario
- Start mux server with a test `wezterm.lua` that includes the forwarding snippet (from our template).
- Spawn a dummy pane that emits a `SetUserVar` escape sequence with a base64 JSON payload.
- Verify that:
  - WezTerm triggers `user-var-changed`
  - `wezterm.background_child_process` runs `wa event ...`
  - watcher receives the IPC message and records/logs it

## Assertions
- Watcher logs show "signal received" with pane_id and name.
- Optional: watcher updates pane state or persists a `pane_signals` record.

## Artifacts/logging
- Capture wezterm logs and wa logs.

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Script is deterministic on a dev machine.
- On failure, artifacts show whether the break is:
  - lua snippet not installed
  - wa event IPC
  - watcher receiver


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Ensure the test fails if the payload is malformed (prove input validation exists) and produces a clear error artifact.
  - Ensure the test fails if the user-var is emitted but not forwarded (lua lane broken).

- Artifact validation:
  - Assert wezterm logs and wa watcher logs are both captured and include correlated timestamps/pane ids.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.15","title":"E2E script: wa watch graceful shutdown (SIGINT flush, lock release, restart clean)","description":"# Task: E2E script — graceful shutdown and restart

## Goal
Validate that `wa watch` behaves safely under real-world stop/restart cycles.

This test guards against a class of user-hostile failures:
- Ctrl-C leaves the DB writer queue half-flushed
- lock files are left behind and prevent restart
- partial writes break FTS or schema invariants

## Scenario
- Start a mux server and spawn a dummy pane that prints a predictable token stream.
- Start `wa watch` with verbose logging and a dedicated workspace.
- Wait until at least one segment is persisted (FTS finds a unique token).
- Send SIGINT to `wa watch`.

## Assertions
- `wa watch` exits cleanly within a bounded timeout.
- Storage is flushed:
  - FTS still works after shutdown (search finds previously written token)
- Lock is released:
  - restarting `wa watch` in the same workspace succeeds

## Artifacts
- Same artifacts contract as `wa-4vx.10.6`, plus:
  - shutdown summary section
  - restart attempt logs

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic.
- On failure, artifacts make it obvious whether the bug is:
  - signal handling
  - storage flush
  - lock release
  - FTS corruption


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add a deliberate failure mode (e.g., kill -9) in a separate variant to confirm the test distinguishes graceful vs abrupt termination.
  - Ensure the test fails if the lock is not released (second watcher start must be an assertion, not “best effort”).

- Artifact validation:
  - Assert the artifacts include the shutdown/restart summary sections and that FTS queries are recorded as evidence.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.16","title":"E2E script: RequireApproval → wa approve allow-once → send succeeds (audited)","description":"# Task: E2E script — approval allow-once

## Goal
Validate the full RequireApproval UX loop:
1) an action requires approval
2) wa returns a structured allow-once payload
3) a human grants approval via `wa approve`
4) the action succeeds on retry
5) the whole flow is auditable and redacted

This is the critical "human-in-the-loop" bridge that keeps wa safe and ergonomic.

## Scenario
- Use a policy config that forces RequireApproval for SendText in a safe situation (so this test is deterministic).
- Spawn a dummy pane that echoes received input.

Steps
1. Attempt a send (`wa send` or `wa robot send`): expect RequireApproval.
2. Extract `allow_once_code` from the output.
3. Run `wa approve <code> --yes`.
4. Retry the same send: expect Allow and successful injection.
5. Verify audit trail contains:
   - initial require-approval decision
   - approval grant
   - successful retry
   - redacted summaries (no raw secret-like input)

## Assertions
- RequireApproval returns a stable allow-once payload.
- Approval is scoped (wrong pane/action/workspace does not match).
- Retry succeeds only for the matching fingerprint.

## Artifacts
- Effective config used.
- Robot/human command outputs.
- Audit export JSONL (or query output).

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Script is deterministic.
- On failure, artifacts clearly distinguish:
  - policy decision bug
  - approval storage/lookup bug
  - audit/redaction bug


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add a negative assertion: use the allow-once code against a different pane/workspace and ensure it is rejected.
  - If approvals have TTL, assert that an expired token is rejected (or explicitly document if TTL is not implemented).

- Artifact validation:
  - Assert audit artifacts include three distinct records (require-approval, approve, allowed retry) and are fully redacted.

-{"id":"wa-4vx.10.17","title":"E2E script: pane exclude filter prevents capture (ignored pane absent from search, shown in status)","description":"# Task: E2E script — pane exclude filter

## Goal
Validate that pane selection filters protect privacy and behave predictably:
- excluded panes are visible (as ignored) but not ingested
- excluded panes do not appear in search results

## Scenario
- Spawn two dummy panes:
  1) "observed" pane prints OBSERVED_TOKEN
  2) "ignored" pane prints SECRET_TOKEN and has a title/cwd that matches an exclude rule

- Configure wa with an exclude rule that matches the ignored pane.

Steps
1) Start wa watch with the configured workspace.
2) Wait until the observed token is persisted (via wa robot search).
3) Assert that searching for SECRET_TOKEN returns no results.
4) Assert wa status / wa robot state shows the ignored pane as ignored with a reason.

## Assertions
- Observed pane is captured and searchable.
- Ignored pane is not captured and not searchable.
- Status output makes the ignore decision obvious.

## Artifacts
- Effective config used (including pane rules).
- wa watch logs with decision messages (no secrets).
- wa robot state JSON.
- wa robot search JSON for both tokens.

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic.
- On failure, artifacts show whether the bug is:
  - filter matching
  - ingest mistakenly tailing ignored panes
  - search indexing
  - status/state reporting


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Assert that SECRET_TOKEN never appears in artifacts/logs (privacy guarantee), not just “not searchable”.
  - Add a control assertion that OBSERVED_TOKEN does appear in search to avoid false negatives.

- Artifact validation:
  - Assert state/status artifacts include the ignore reason so the user can understand why capture was skipped.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.18","title":"E2E script: secret redaction in audit/export (no raw secrets in artifacts)","description":"# Task: E2E script — secret redaction in audit/export

## Goal
Prove that end-to-end audit and export paths never leak secrets from action inputs.

This is a trust-critical property:
- users will paste artifacts into issues
- CI will upload artifacts
- we must ensure secrets are not present in these outputs

## Scenario
- Spawn a dummy pane that echoes received input.
- Ensure policy allows a send in a safe context.

Steps
1) Send a string containing a known fake secret pattern (e.g., `sk-FAKESECRET123`) via `wa send` or `wa robot send`.
2) Export audit trail slice for this run (or query audit feed).
3) Assert that:
  - the audit entry exists
  - the raw secret string does NOT appear anywhere in audit export output
  - a redacted placeholder DOES appear (to prove redaction happened, not omission)

## Assertions
- Audit emission records the action.
- Redaction rules remove the secret from:
  - audit DB row(s)
  - audit export output
  - E2E script logs + artifacts

## Artifacts
- Effective config used.
- Audit export JSONL.
- Script stdout/stderr.
- Grep-style proof output (counts of secret matches should be 0).

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- The test is deterministic.
- Any failure clearly indicates whether the leak came from:
  - audit emission
  - redaction engine
  - export path
  - e2e script logging


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - The script must scan *all* produced artifacts (logs, JSONL exports, summaries) for the fake secret string.
  - Include at least two different fake secret patterns (OpenAI-style, GitHub token-style) to ensure redactor coverage isn’t overly narrow.

- Failure mode:
  - If a match is found, print the exact artifact filename + surrounding context (bounded) to make the leak diagnosable.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.19","title":"E2E script: workspace isolation (no cross-project DB leakage; --workspace honored)","description":"# Task: E2E script — workspace isolation

## Goal
Prove that wa honors workspace scoping and does not leak data across projects.

This is a critical UX property:
- users run multiple projects in parallel
- agents should not read/write the wrong DB
- debugging depends on knowing which workspace you are looking at

## Scenario
- Spawn a dummy pane that prints a unique token: WORKSPACE_TOKEN.

Steps
1) Start wa watch with --workspace <A>.
2) Wait until WORKSPACE_TOKEN is persisted (wa robot search --workspace <A>).
3) Run wa robot search --workspace <B> for WORKSPACE_TOKEN.

Assertions
- In workspace A: the token is found.
- In workspace B: the token is NOT found.

Optional extension
- Start wa watch in workspace B and ensure it creates a distinct DB.

## Artifacts
- effective config for both workspaces
- wa watch logs
- wa robot search outputs for A and B

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic.
- Failures make it obvious whether the bug is:
  - workspace resolution
  - DB path derivation
  - CLI flag propagation


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Assert the two workspaces produce distinct DB/log paths (not just search behavior).
  - Add a control assertion that workspace B can find a different token once it has ingested something.

- Artifact validation:
  - Artifacts must include the derived paths (workspace root, db path, log path) to make mis-scoping diagnosable.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.2","title":"Criterion benchmarks + budgets (pattern detect, delta extraction, FTS query)","description":"# Task: Benchmarks + budgets

## Goal
Prevent performance regressions on hot paths.

This is the “performance test suite” complement to unit/E2E tests: it ensures wa stays fast enough to run continuously.

## Bench targets (must exist)
- Overlap/delta extraction (hot ingest path)
- Pattern engine quick reject (typical no-match)
- Pattern detection on “typical pane output”
- FTS query latency for common queries on representative DB size
- Watcher loop overhead when idle (per-pane check cost)

## Initial budgets (PLAN §13.4 + Appendix G.7)
These are v0 targets; we can tune later, but they must be explicitly recorded so regressions are real failures, not vibes.

- Quick reject no-match: **< 1µs** for typical non-matching text.
- Pattern detection (typical corpus): **p50 < 1ms**, **p99 < 5ms**.
- FTS query common patterns (DB ~100k captures):
  - **p50 < 10ms**, **p99 < 50ms** (hard cap: < 50ms for common queries).
- Watcher loop overhead (idle): **< 100µs per pane check**.

Notes:
- Budgets should be reported with machine metadata (OS/CPU/rustc/features).
- CI should enforce budgets in a non-flaky way (relative threshold vs baseline, or dedicated perf runners).

## CI integration
- Local-only initially is acceptable if CI is too noisy, but the harness must produce machine-readable artifacts.
- Once stable, integrate into CI (tracked by `wa-nu4.3.4.4`) with:
  - clear pass/fail thresholds
  - artifact upload of benchmark results on failure

## Artifacts & logging
Bench runs must produce debuggable artifacts:
- raw Criterion JSON output
- a short summary (top regressions, top slowest benches)
- build/machine metadata:
  - OS, CPU model, rustc version, enabled features
  - git commit hash when available

## Testing
- Bench harness self-check:
  - Add a small “sanity bench” guaranteed to run so the harness failing to execute is caught early.

- Budget enforcement tests:
  - If budget checks are implemented as code, include a unit test that feeds a fake benchmark JSON and asserts:
    - regression triggers failure
    - outputs include which benchmark regressed and by how much

## Acceptance Criteria
- Bench suite runs locally and produces a baseline.
- Budgets above are documented and emitted in summaries.
- CI enforcement can come later if early CI is too noisy, but the artifacts/logging are in place.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.3","title":"[EPIC] Storage & indexing (SQLite WAL + FTS5 + async writer)","description":"# Storage & indexing — SQLite WAL + FTS5

## Goal
Persist:
- raw pane output as **append-only segments** (delta-first)
- explicit `GAP` events when continuity cannot be guaranteed
- detected events + extracted facts
- workflow execution traces (step logs) for resumability

and provide fast query surfaces:
- full-text search (FTS5) across all captured output
- scoped search per pane/domain/time

## Rationale
The database is wa’s "world memory". It enables:
- debugging (what happened?)
- agent coordination (search + recall)
- workflow correctness (structured facts, resumability)

We choose SQLite because:
- zero-config, embeddable, portable
- supports WAL for concurrent readers + single writer
- FTS5 is excellent for terminal transcript search

## Architectural constraints
- `rusqlite::Connection` is not `Send` and cannot be used directly inside async tasks.
- We must not block the async runtime with DB writes.

Therefore the design is:
- **single dedicated writer thread** receiving bounded write commands
- **small read-only connection pool** for queries (WAL allows concurrent reads)

## Deliverables
- Schema and migrations strategy (even if v0 is "init schema only"):
  - domains, panes
  - output segments (seq monotonic per pane)
  - output gaps (explicit discontinuities)
  - FTS virtual table + triggers
  - events (detections)
  - agent sessions (token usage, session IDs, etc.)
  - workflow executions + step logs
  - accounts + config + maintenance log
- `StorageHandle` async API with:
  - `append_segment`, `record_gap`, `record_event`
  - `search(fts_query, options)` with correct bm25 ranking
  - `workflow_step_*` methods for durable step logs
- Retention + hygiene mechanisms:
  - retention-by-age and/or size
  - explicit `vacuum` command only

## Acceptance
- `wa` can write segments and query them back.
- FTS finds known text quickly.
- Writer thread enforces per-pane seq monotonicity and emits/records gaps when violated.



## Success Criteria
- SQLite schema + migrations exist and are exercised by tests.
- StorageHandle provides single-writer semantics and supports concurrent read queries without blocking the async runtime.
- FTS search works end-to-end (segments written → indexed → searchable) with deterministic scoping.
- Core storage invariants are enforced (seq monotonicity, explicit gaps, audit/event/workflow trace durability).
- Unit/integration tests cover writer queue behavior, migrations, FTS queries, and workflow/audit storage paths.


## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Schema/migration roundtrips and “invariant checks” (seq monotonicity, explicit gaps, FK consistency).
  - Writer queue behavior under load (bounded queue backpressure, graceful shutdown flush).
  - FTS query correctness and scoping (pane/domain/time) including snippet/highlight determinism.

- Integration tests:
  - End-to-end “write segments → index → search” with deterministic fixtures.
  - Restart/resume: open DB, write, close, reopen, verify writer thread state rehydrates correctly.

- Property/fuzz tests (as additive confidence for correctness):
  - Proptest seq generation ensures monotonic enforcement and correct GAP insertion semantics (`wa-4vx.10.5`).
  - Fuzz inputs for query parsing / escaping / injection-hardening (`wa-4vx.10.3`).

- Artifact/log requirements (for integration/E2E):
  - On failure, dump the minimal DB snapshot or deterministic query output needed to reproduce.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Deliverables.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.3.1","title":"Define SQLite schema (segments+gaps+events+workflows+FTS) + init/migration strategy","description":"# Task: Define SQLite schema + init/migration strategy

## Goal
Define the database schema that supports:
- append-only output segments (delta-first)
- explicit output gaps
- panes metadata + observation status (observed vs ignored)
- events (detections) with extracted JSON
- event lifecycle: unhandled vs handled, workflow linkage, idempotency/dedupe
- agent sessions and token usage
- workflow executions + step logs (durability/resumability)
- accounts mirror (usage tracking)
- config + maintenance log
- (later) audit trail of actions (see wa-4vx.3.8)

## Rationale
DB schema mistakes are expensive. We want a schema that:
- is append-friendly (write-optimized)
- is easy to query for recent state
- enables FTS for fast recall
- supports workflow resumption after crash
- supports user trust: we can explain why wa acted (events + workflows + audit)

## Required properties
- WAL mode, foreign_keys on.
- output_segments has UNIQUE(pane_id, seq).
- GAPs are explicit (output_gaps).
- FTS5 virtual table over output_segments + triggers.

## Panes table requirements
We need a panes table to support:
- stable UI/state surfaces (wa status / wa robot state)
- observation decisions (privacy/perf)

Minimum fields (names flexible):
- pane_id (and domain/window/tab identifiers)
- title, cwd
- last_seen_at
- observed: bool
- ignore_reason: nullable short string (rule id/name)
- last_decision_at

## Event lifecycle requirements
We need to support:
- events that start as unhandled
- linkage to a handling attempt:
  - handled_by_workflow_execution_id (nullable)
  - handled_at (nullable)
  - handled_status (completed/aborted/failed/paused per semantics)
- idempotency/deduping:
  - optional dedupe_key (pane_id + rule_id + window) to prevent duplicates

Exact column naming is flexible, but the schema must make:
- `wa events --unhandled` cheap
- marking handled a single write

## Deliverables
- SCHEMA string (or migrations folder if we choose migrations now).
- Documented conventions:
  - timestamps: use epoch ms for hot paths (captured_at int)
  - JSON columns are TEXT containing JSON (v0)
  - indexing strategy for common queries

## Testing
- Schema/migration tests (see `wa-4vx.3.10`):
  - create fresh DB
  - upgrade from prior schema versions
  - validate core invariants and indexes exist
- Storage tests (see `wa-4vx.3.7`):
  - insert/query round-trips for segments/gaps/events/workflows

## Acceptance Criteria
- DB initializes successfully on first run.
- Basic inserts into segments/events/panes succeed.
- We can mark an event as handled and query unhandled events efficiently.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.3.10","title":"SQLite migrations & upgrade tests: schema versioning, forward upgrades, invariants","description":"# Task: SQLite migrations & upgrade tests

## Goal
Ensure DB schema evolution is safe and predictable.

Even though we do not care about long-term backwards compatibility, we DO care about:
- wa users upgrading from yesterday's build to today's build
- avoiding "my DB is bricked" situations
- making schema changes intentional and test-backed

## Strategy
- Use an explicit schema version (e.g., `PRAGMA user_version`) and a clear migration runner.
- Keep a minimal set of historical fixtures for N-1 and N-2 schema versions.

## Testing (minimum)
1. Fresh init
   - new empty workspace initializes DB
   - required tables/indexes exist
   - WAL + foreign_keys configured

2. Upgrade path
   - start from an older fixture DB
   - run wa startup migration path
   - verify:
     - user_version bumped correctly
     - no required tables are missing
     - FTS tables/triggers exist and are functional

3. Invariants
   - output_segments `UNIQUE(pane_id, seq)` enforced
   - events lifecycle columns exist and unhandled queries are indexed/fast

## Diagnostics on failure
- Print clear errors:
  - expected vs actual user_version
  - missing table/index names
  - migration step that failed

## Acceptance Criteria
- A contributor can safely change schema by updating migrations AND updating these tests.
- CI catches broken migrations before users do.

-{"id":"wa-4vx.3.11","title":"Data volume sizing + compression decision gate","description":"# Task: Data volume sizing + compression decision gate

## Goal
Answer the open question: is retention alone sufficient, or do we need compression in v0.1/v0.2?

## Why
We need predictable disk usage and query performance as pane counts grow.
A data-volume study prevents surprises and avoids premature complexity.

## Work items
- Build a synthetic capture workload:
  - N panes (e.g., 20/50/100)
  - realistic output rate (lines/sec)
  - typical session duration (1h, 8h, 24h)
- Measure:
  - DB growth rate
  - FTS insert performance
  - query latency at 1M / 10M / 50M rows
- Decide:
  - keep retention-only, OR
  - add compression (and where: segments table, cold storage, export-only)

## Deliverables
- A sizing table and recommendation.
- If compression is required, create follow-up beads for:
  - compression format
  - migration strategy
  - query impact

## Testing
- Add a repeatable benchmark harness (can be a test-only helper) that generates synthetic segments and reports sizes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Decision recorded in this bead with rationale and thresholds (e.g., "if DB > 1GB in 30 days, add compression").

 {"id":"wa-4vx.3.2","title":"Implement StorageHandle (single writer thread + read pool) with bounded write queue","description":"# Task: Implement StorageHandle async architecture

## Goal
Provide an async-safe storage API:
- never blocks the Tokio runtime on SQLite I/O
- centralizes all writes through one dedicated writer thread
- allows concurrent reads via small read-only pool (WAL)

## Why
`rusqlite::Connection` is not Send/Sync; direct use inside async tasks causes runtime stalls.

## Workspace/path behavior
- StorageHandle should be constructed from a resolved workspace/config path (`wa-4vx.9.2` / `wa-4vx.9.6`).
- It must create parent directories for the DB path if missing.

## Deliverables
- StorageHandle with:
  - bounded mpsc channel for write commands (backpressure)
  - writer thread main loop
  - read pool via deadpool_sqlite (or equivalent)
- Clear shutdown path:
  - stop accepting new writes
  - flush queued writes
  - close connections

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests (see `wa-4vx.3.7`):
  - bounded queue enforces backpressure (no unbounded growth)
  - writer thread serializes writes correctly
  - concurrent reads function while writes are happening (WAL)
  - shutdown flushes pending writes deterministically
  - failure modes (DB locked/unwritable) yield actionable errors (no panics)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Writes succeed under load without blocking async tasks.
- Reads can run concurrently while writes happen.
- On shutdown, queued writes are flushed deterministically.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.3.3","title":"Persist output segments + enforce per-pane seq monotonicity; record gaps on discontinuity","description":"# Task: Persist output segments + seq monotonicity + gaps

## Goal
Persist the ingest delta stream as append-only rows:
- output_segments (pane_id, seq, captured_at, text)
- output_gaps (pane_id, seq_range, reason)

## Why
This is the foundation for:
- FTS search
- deterministic event reconstruction
- workflow resumption and verification

## Requirements
- Enforce seq monotonicity:
  - UNIQUE(pane_id, seq)
  - reject or record inconsistency with a gap (depending on decision)
- Record gaps when:
  - overlap detection fails
  - cursor jumps / truncation detected
  - seq discontinuity detected

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests (see `wa-4vx.3.7` and `wa-4vx.4.8`):
  - inserting duplicate `(pane_id, seq)` fails predictably
  - discontinuities create explicit gap rows with a stable reason
  - last-N queries return deterministic ordering and are indexed
  - no panics on malformed input; errors are actionable

## Acceptance Criteria
- Inserting segments with the same (pane_id, seq) fails predictably.
- Gap rows are created for overlap failures and discontinuities.
- Queries can fetch the last N segments for a pane efficiently.

  - `wa-4vx.10.2` adds benchmark coverage for common queries and budgets regressions

- Invalid FTS syntax returns a structured, actionable error.
- Ranking/order is stable under deterministic fixtures.

 {"id":"wa-am5","title":"E2E script: dry-run mode (wa send/workflow --dry-run previews)","description":"# E2E script: dry-run mode (previews for wa send / wa workflow / robot)

## Goal
Validate end-to-end that `--dry-run` produces accurate previews **without mutating anything**:
- correct target resolution (pane/domain/cwd/agent)
- correct policy evaluation (allow/deny/require-approval)
- correct “what would happen” step plan for workflows
- deterministic, schema-valid JSON output for machines

This is the integration proof for the dry-run epic (`wa-1pe`) using the shared E2E harness.

## Why this matters
Dry-run is the primary trust-building UX feature:
- users can explore safely
- robots can plan before acting
- reduces mistakes and support burden

## Key constraints
- **Zero side effects**:
  - must not send input to panes
  - must not acquire workflow locks
  - must not mark events handled
  - must not create workflow executions
  - may optionally write an audit entry tagged as a *dry-run evaluation* (explicitly non-action)
- **Deterministic**: no fixed sleeps; use bounded waits/quiescence.
- **Machine-stable**: tests should prefer `--format json` outputs (avoid scraping rich/TTY output).
- Uses the standard harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).

## Test setup
- Start a WezTerm mux server and spawn dummy panes that:
  - can echo any received input to prove whether a send occurred
  - can deterministically emit patterns to create events for workflow previews
- Run `wa watch` in an isolated E2E workspace.

## Scenarios

### 1) `wa send --dry-run` preview quality
- Run: `wa send --pane 3 --dry-run "hello" --format json`
- Assert preview includes:
  - resolved pane info (pane_id, title, cwd, agent)
  - policy decision + reasons (capabilities, safety gate)
  - an explicit “would send” description (bytes/line endings/control codes)
  - an explicit “would wait-for” plan if applicable

### 2) `wa send --dry-run` on a denied state
- Create a deterministic unsafe state (fixture / dummy alt-screen).
- Run: `wa send --pane 3 --dry-run "hello" --format json`
- Assert:
  - decision is deny/require-approval as expected
  - the preview explains remediation (e.g., exit AltScreen)
  - **no text was sent** (dummy pane echo confirms)

### 3) Preview vs actual: stable-field match
Goal: the preview’s *decision* and *target* must match the actual path.
- Capture preview JSON: `wa send … --dry-run --format json > artifacts/send_preview.json`
- Execute actual send: `wa send … --format json > artifacts/send_actual.json`
- Compare only stable fields:
  - `target.pane_id`
  - `policy.decision` + `policy.rule_id` (if present)
  - `action_kind`
  - (ignore timestamps, durations, correlation ids)

### 4) `wa workflow run --dry-run` step plan preview
- Ensure a deterministic unhandled event exists (dummy pane emits marker).
- Run: `wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 3 --dry-run --format json`
- Assert preview includes:
  - step list in order (with step ids/names)
  - per-step policy checks (or a summarized policy gate)
  - expected waits/verifications
  - explicit note that no locks will be acquired and no DB state will change

### 5) Robot dry-run output (schema + no mutation)
- Run: `wa robot send --pane 3 --dry-run "hello" --format json`
- Run: `wa robot workflow handle_compaction 3 --dry-run --format json` (or the canonical robot workflow invocation)
- Assert outputs validate against the dry-run JSON schemas and include:
  - `decision` / `would_execute=false`
  - `steps_preview` (for workflows)

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`) with prerequisites and default inclusion status.

## Artifacts
- `send_preview.json`
- `send_actual.json`
- `workflow_preview.json`
- `dry_run_vs_actual.json` (comparison result)
- `wa_watch.log`
- `events.jsonl`
- `audit_slice.jsonl` (to prove “no action” vs “action”)

## Logging contract
Log per scenario:
- command invoked (redacted)
- timeout settings
- comparison summary (stable fields)

Example:
```
[DRYRUN_E2E] scenario=send_preview pane=3
[DRYRUN_E2E] decision=allow would_execute=false
[DRYRUN_E2E] scenario=preview_vs_actual stable_match=true
```

## Testing
- Determinism:
  - no fixed sleeps
  - every wait has a timeout and emits actionable failure output
- No-mutation proofs (pick at least two independent signals):
  - dummy pane did not echo received input
  - no new `send_text` audit action exists (only optional `dry_run` audit)
  - no `workflow_executions` row was created on dry-run
- Schema:
  - JSON outputs validate against the documented dry-run schema(s)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Dry-run output is informative (target + policy + plan).
- [ ] Dry-run never mutates panes/locks/handled state.
- [ ] Preview decisions match actual decisions on stable fields.
- [ ] Robot dry-run output is schema-valid and deterministic.

 {"id":"wa-bnm","title":"[EPIC] Actionable Quick-Fix Suggestions for Events and Errors","description":"# [EPIC] Actionable Quick-Fix Suggestions

## Mission
Every detected event and every error includes an **explicit, copy-pasteable command** to fix or handle it. No ambiguity about what to do next.

## Why This Matters
When something happens—an event is detected, an error occurs—users face a "now what?" moment:
- "Which command handles this event?"
- "How do I fix this error?"
- "What are my options?"

Quick-fix suggestions eliminate this friction:
- Copy-paste the suggested command
- See alternative approaches
- Learn the correct workflow

## How It Works

### For Events
```bash
$ wa events --unhandled

Unhandled Events (2):

1. codex.usage_limit_reached (Pane 9, 2 min ago)
   Codex hit its usage limit and needs account rotation.
   
   Quick fix: wa workflow run handle_usage_limits --pane 9
   Preview:   wa workflow run handle_usage_limits --pane 9 --dry-run
   Manual:    Exit Codex (Ctrl-C), log in with different account

2. session.compaction (Pane 3, 5 min ago)
   Claude Code compacted context and may have lost project state.
   
   Quick fix: wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 3
   Preview:   wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 3 --dry-run
   Manual:    Ask Claude to re-read AGENTS.md and key project files
```

### For Errors
```bash
$ wa watch
Error: WezTerm CLI not found

Quick fix: brew install wezterm  # macOS
           apt install wezterm   # Ubuntu
           See: https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/install

Then retry: wa watch
```

```bash
$ wa send --pane 99 "hello"
Error: Pane 99 not found

Available panes: 1, 3, 7, 9
Did you mean:   wa send --pane 9 "hello"  (closest match)
List all:       wa status
```

### For Policy Denials
```bash
$ wa send --pane 3 "continue"
Error: Send denied - AltScreen active

Quick fix: Exit AltScreen (close vim/less/etc.) and retry
Why:       wa why denied --pane 3
```

## Implementation Components

### 1. Event Suggestion Templates
Each rule definition includes `suggested_action`:
```rust
RuleDef {
    id: "codex.usage_limit_reached",
    suggested_workflow: Some("handle_usage_limits"),
    manual_fix: "Exit Codex (Ctrl-C), log in with different account",
    // ...
}
```

### 2. Error Remediation System
Error types carry remediation hints:
```rust
enum WaError {
    WezTermNotFound {
        remediation: vec![
            Remediation::Command("brew install wezterm"),
            Remediation::Url("https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/install"),
        ]
    },
    // ...
}
```

### 3. Context-Aware Suggestions
Use current state to generate relevant commands:
- Typo detection ("Did you mean pane 9?")
- Available resources ("Available panes: 1, 3, 7, 9")
- Environment-specific hints (macOS vs Linux)

## Design Principles
1. **Always show the command**: Not "run the workflow" but `wa workflow run X`
2. **Copy-friendly**: Commands can be copy-pasted directly
3. **Show alternatives**: Quick fix, preview, manual
4. **Progressive detail**: Brief first, expand on request

## Testing
- Unit tests: Each rule has valid suggestion templates
- Integration tests: Suggestions are generated for known scenarios
- UX tests: Suggestions are understandable and accurate

## Success Criteria
- All unhandled events include suggested commands
- All errors include "Quick fix:" with specific steps
- Suggestions are context-aware (show available panes, etc.)
- Users can copy-paste suggestions directly

## Acceptance Criteria
- Actionable suggestions appear for common error and event types.
- Suggestions are context-aware, redacted, and non-spammy.
- Suggested actions include stable IDs and references to fixes.
- All child tasks are complete and wa-bnm.4 tests pass.

 {"id":"wa-bnm.2","title":"Error remediation system: WaError types carry actionable fixes","description":"
# Error Remediation System

## Purpose
Make every error in wa self-documenting with specific, actionable remediation steps.

## Implementation

### Remediation Struct
```rust
pub struct Remediation {
    pub summary: String,           // One-line fix
    pub commands: Vec<RemediationCommand>,
    pub alternatives: Vec<String>,
    pub learn_more: Option<String>,
}

pub struct RemediationCommand {
    pub label: String,        // "Quick fix", "Preview", "Override"
    pub command: String,      // Actual command
    pub platform: Option<String>, // "macOS", "Linux", etc.
}
```

### Error Types with Remediation
```rust
pub enum WaError {
    WezTermNotFound(Remediation),
    PaneNotFound { pane_id: PaneId, available: Vec<PaneId>, remediation: Remediation },
    PolicyDenied { reason: PolicyReason, remediation: Remediation },
    ConfigInvalid { field: String, remediation: Remediation },
    // ...
}
```

### Platform Detection
Detect OS to provide platform-specific commands:
```
macOS: brew install wezterm
Ubuntu: apt install wezterm
Arch: pacman -S wezterm
```

## Display Format
```
Error: WezTerm CLI not found

Quick fix:
  macOS:  brew install wezterm
  Ubuntu: apt install wezterm
  Other:  https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/install

Then retry: wa watch
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All WaError variants include remediation
- [ ] Platform-specific commands where relevant
- [ ] Remediation is always copy-pasteable
- [ ] Error display includes remediation by default

## Testing
- Unit tests for template rendering and suggested_action interpolation.
- Integration tests: fixture errors/events produce actionable suggestions.
- E2E: extend an events/error scenario to assert suggestions and rule-id logs.

 {"id":"wa-bnm.3","title":"Context-aware suggestions: typo detection, fuzzy matching, available resources","description":"
# Context-Aware Suggestions

## Purpose
Use current system state to provide smart, contextual suggestions in error messages.

## Features

### 1. Typo Detection
When user specifies non-existent resource, suggest closest match:
```
$ wa send --pane 99 "hello"
Error: Pane 99 not found

Did you mean: wa send --pane 9 "hello"  (closest match)
Available:    1, 3, 7, 9
```

Uses Levenshtein distance for string matching.

### 2. Available Resources
Always show what's available when something is not found:
```
Available panes: 1 (codex), 3 (claude), 7 (local)
Available workflows: handle_compaction, handle_usage_limits
Available rules: codex.*, claude.*, core.*
```

### 3. Environment-Aware Commands
Detect environment and tailor suggestions:
- macOS: homebrew commands
- Linux: apt/dnf/pacman commands
- Container: different paths

### 4. Recent State Hints
```
$ wa send --pane 3 "continue"
Error: Pane 3 is in AltScreen (vim)

Hint: Pane 3 entered AltScreen 2 minutes ago
Wait for: vim to exit, or use a different pane
```

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct SuggestionContext {
    pub available_panes: Vec<PaneInfo>,
    pub available_workflows: Vec<String>,
    pub platform: Platform,
    pub recent_state: Vec<StateChange>,
}

pub fn suggest_closest<T>(input: &str, candidates: &[T]) -> Option<T>;
pub fn format_available(items: &[T]) -> String;
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Typo suggestions for pane IDs
- [ ] Available resources shown on not-found errors
- [ ] Platform-specific commands detected automatically
- [ ] Recent state provides temporal context

## Testing
- Unit tests for template rendering and suggested_action interpolation.
- Integration tests: fixture errors/events produce actionable suggestions.
- E2E: extend an events/error scenario to assert suggestions and rule-id logs.

# Quick-Fix Testing Suite

## Purpose
Ensure all suggestions are accurate, copy-pasteable, and helpful.

## Test Categories

### 1. Template Interpolation Tests
- {pane} substitution works
- {event_id} substitution works
- Invalid templates fail gracefully

### 2. Error Remediation Coverage
- Every WaError variant has remediation
- Remediations are non-empty
- Commands are syntactically valid

### 3. UX Validation Tests
- Commands can be copy-pasted (no smart quotes, etc.)
- Commands are actually runnable
- Suggestions match actual system state

### 4. Context-Aware Tests
- Typo detection finds correct suggestions
- Available resources are accurate
- Platform detection is correct

### 5. Snapshot Tests
- Error message format stability
- Suggestion format stability

## Test Fixtures
- Error scenarios with known remediations
- Event scenarios with known suggestions
- Platform-specific command sets

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 100% of error types have remediation tests
- [ ] 100% of rule types have suggestion tests
- [ ] Snapshot tests for output stability
- [ ] Copy-paste validation (no encoding issues)

## Testing
- Unit tests for template rendering and suggested_action interpolation.
- Integration tests: fixture errors/events produce actionable suggestions.
- E2E: extend an events/error scenario to assert suggestions and rule-id logs.
